instruction: Compare C++ error handling approaches: exceptions vs error codes vs std::expected. When should I use each?
output:
<think>
C++ has three main error handling philosophies, each with tradeoffs:

1. Exceptions: Zero-cost happy path, expensive throw path. Good for truly exceptional situations. Problems: can't use in embedded/kernel, hard to reason about exception safety, hidden control flow.

2. Error codes (C-style or enum): Explicit, no hidden control flow, works everywhere. Problems: easy to ignore, clutters return types, no stack unwinding.

3. std::expected (C++23): Best of both — typed return value that's either T or error E. Like Rust's Result. Explicit, can't be ignored, no exception overhead.

Decision criteria:
- Performance-critical hot path: std::expected or error codes (no exception overhead)
- Constructors/operators: exceptions (can't return error codes)
- Embedded/kernel/real-time: error codes or expected (no exception support)
- Library API: std::expected (modern, explicit, composable)
- Truly exceptional cases (out of memory, corrupted state): exceptions

I'll show all three approaches for the same problem: file parsing.
</think>

```cpp
#include <expected>    // C++23
#include <fstream>
#include <optional>
#include <string>
#include <system_error>
#include <variant>
#include <vector>
#include <iostream>

// === Approach 1: Exceptions (traditional C++) ===

namespace exc {

struct ParseError : std::runtime_error {
    int line;
    ParseError(const std::string& msg, int line)
        : std::runtime_error(msg), line(line) {}
};

struct Config {
    std::string host;
    int port;
};

Config parse_config(const std::string& path) {
    std::ifstream file(path);
    if (!file.is_open()) {
        throw std::runtime_error("Cannot open: " + path);
    }
    Config cfg;
    std::string line;
    int line_num = 0;
    while (std::getline(file, line)) {
        ++line_num;
        auto eq = line.find('=');
        if (eq == std::string::npos) {
            throw ParseError("Missing '=' on line " + std::to_string(line_num), line_num);
        }
        auto key = line.substr(0, eq);
        auto val = line.substr(eq + 1);
        if (key == "host") cfg.host = val;
        else if (key == "port") cfg.port = std::stoi(val);
    }
    return cfg;
}

void demo() {
    try {
        auto cfg = parse_config("config.txt");
        std::cout << cfg.host << ":" << cfg.port << "\n";
    } catch (const ParseError& e) {
        std::cerr << "Parse error at line " << e.line << ": " << e.what() << "\n";
    } catch (const std::exception& e) {
        std::cerr << "Error: " << e.what() << "\n";
    }
}

} // namespace exc


// === Approach 2: Error codes (C-style, explicit) ===

namespace errc {

enum class ConfigError {
    Ok = 0,
    FileNotFound,
    ParseError,
    InvalidPort,
};

struct Config {
    std::string host;
    int port;
};

ConfigError parse_config(const std::string& path, Config& out) {
    std::ifstream file(path);
    if (!file.is_open()) return ConfigError::FileNotFound;
    std::string line;
    while (std::getline(file, line)) {
        auto eq = line.find('=');
        if (eq == std::string::npos) return ConfigError::ParseError;
        auto key = line.substr(0, eq);
        auto val = line.substr(eq + 1);
        if (key == "host") out.host = val;
        else if (key == "port") {
            try { out.port = std::stoi(val); }
            catch (...) { return ConfigError::InvalidPort; }
        }
    }
    return ConfigError::Ok;
}

void demo() {
    Config cfg;
    auto err = parse_config("config.txt", cfg);
    if (err != ConfigError::Ok) {
        std::cerr << "Error code: " << static_cast<int>(err) << "\n";
        return;
    }
    std::cout << cfg.host << ":" << cfg.port << "\n";
}

} // namespace errc


// === Approach 3: std::expected (C++23, recommended) ===

namespace exp {

struct ConfigError {
    enum Kind { FileNotFound, ParseError, InvalidPort };
    Kind kind;
    std::string detail;
    int line = 0;
};

struct Config {
    std::string host;
    int port;
};

std::expected<Config, ConfigError> parse_config(const std::string& path) {
    std::ifstream file(path);
    if (!file.is_open()) {
        return std::unexpected(ConfigError{
            ConfigError::FileNotFound, "Cannot open: " + path
        });
    }
    Config cfg;
    std::string line;
    int line_num = 0;
    while (std::getline(file, line)) {
        ++line_num;
        auto eq = line.find('=');
        if (eq == std::string::npos) {
            return std::unexpected(ConfigError{
                ConfigError::ParseError,
                "Missing '=' on line " + std::to_string(line_num),
                line_num
            });
        }
        auto key = line.substr(0, eq);
        auto val = line.substr(eq + 1);
        if (key == "host") cfg.host = val;
        else if (key == "port") {
            try { cfg.port = std::stoi(val); }
            catch (...) {
                return std::unexpected(ConfigError{
                    ConfigError::InvalidPort, val, line_num
                });
            }
        }
    }
    return cfg;  // Implicit conversion to expected
}

void demo() {
    auto result = parse_config("config.txt");
    if (result) {
        std::cout << result->host << ":" << result->port << "\n";
    } else {
        const auto& err = result.error();
        switch (err.kind) {
            case ConfigError::FileNotFound:
                std::cerr << "File not found: " << err.detail << "\n"; break;
            case ConfigError::ParseError:
                std::cerr << "Parse error at line " << err.line << "\n"; break;
            case ConfigError::InvalidPort:
                std::cerr << "Invalid port: " << err.detail << "\n"; break;
        }
    }

    // Chaining with and_then/transform (C++23)
    auto port = parse_config("config.txt")
        .transform([](const Config& c) { return c.port; })
        .value_or(8080);
}

} // namespace exp

int main() {
    exp::demo();
    return 0;
}
```

instruction: Show how to use noexcept effectively in C++. When should functions be noexcept and what are the consequences?
output:
<think>
noexcept is both a specifier and an operator in C++. Key aspects:

1. As specifier: `void foo() noexcept` promises no exceptions will leave this function. If one does, std::terminate is called (no stack unwinding).

2. Performance: noexcept enables optimizations. Most importantly, std::vector uses move operations ONLY if they're noexcept. Otherwise it copies (for strong exception guarantee).

3. When to use noexcept:
- Move constructors and move assignment (critical for container performance)
- Swap functions
- Destructors (implicitly noexcept already)
- Simple getters/accessors
- Functions that genuinely cannot fail

4. When NOT to use noexcept:
- Functions that might throw in the future (API commitment)
- Functions that call potentially-throwing code
- When you're not sure (wrong noexcept is worse than no noexcept)

5. Conditional noexcept: `noexcept(noexcept(expr))` — noexcept if the expression is noexcept.

The most important rule: move operations MUST be noexcept for performance with standard containers.
</think>

```cpp
#include <algorithm>
#include <iostream>
#include <string>
#include <type_traits>
#include <utility>
#include <vector>

// === Rule 1: Move operations MUST be noexcept ===

class Buffer {
    char* data_;
    size_t size_;

public:
    explicit Buffer(size_t size)
        : data_(new char[size]), size_(size) {}

    ~Buffer() noexcept {  // Destructors are implicitly noexcept
        delete[] data_;
    }

    // Move constructor: noexcept enables vector optimization
    Buffer(Buffer&& other) noexcept
        : data_(std::exchange(other.data_, nullptr))
        , size_(std::exchange(other.size_, 0)) {}

    // Move assignment: noexcept
    Buffer& operator=(Buffer&& other) noexcept {
        if (this != &other) {
            delete[] data_;
            data_ = std::exchange(other.data_, nullptr);
            size_ = std::exchange(other.size_, 0);
        }
        return *this;
    }

    // Copy (might throw — allocation can fail)
    Buffer(const Buffer& other)
        : data_(new char[other.size_]), size_(other.size_) {
        std::copy(other.data_, other.data_ + size_, data_);
    }

    // Swap: always noexcept
    void swap(Buffer& other) noexcept {
        std::swap(data_, other.data_);
        std::swap(size_, other.size_);
    }

    // Simple getters: noexcept
    [[nodiscard]] size_t size() const noexcept { return size_; }
    [[nodiscard]] char* data() noexcept { return data_; }
    [[nodiscard]] const char* data() const noexcept { return data_; }
};

// Without noexcept move, vector::push_back copies instead of moving!
void demo_vector_optimization() {
    std::vector<Buffer> buffers;
    buffers.reserve(1);

    buffers.emplace_back(1024);
    // When vector reallocates, it will MOVE elements (noexcept move exists)
    // Without noexcept move, it would COPY (much slower)
    buffers.emplace_back(2048);  // Triggers reallocation

    // Verify at compile time:
    static_assert(std::is_nothrow_move_constructible_v<Buffer>);
    static_assert(std::is_nothrow_move_assignable_v<Buffer>);
}


// === Rule 2: Conditional noexcept ===

template<typename T>
class Wrapper {
    T value_;
public:
    // noexcept if T's move is noexcept
    Wrapper(Wrapper&& other)
        noexcept(std::is_nothrow_move_constructible_v<T>)
        : value_(std::move(other.value_)) {}

    // noexcept if T's swap is noexcept
    void swap(Wrapper& other)
        noexcept(std::is_nothrow_swappable_v<T>) {
        using std::swap;
        swap(value_, other.value_);
    }

    // Conditional noexcept with noexcept operator
    template<typename F>
    auto transform(F&& fn) noexcept(noexcept(fn(value_)))
        -> decltype(fn(value_)) {
        return fn(value_);
    }
};


// === Rule 3: What NOT to mark noexcept ===

class UserService {
public:
    // DON'T: This allocates (new can throw), does I/O (can throw)
    // std::string find_user(int id) noexcept;  // WRONG!
    std::string find_user(int id);  // Correct: might throw

    // DON'T: Parsing can fail
    // int parse_port(const std::string& s) noexcept;  // WRONG!
    int parse_port(const std::string& s);  // Correct

    // DO: Simple computation, no allocation, no I/O
    [[nodiscard]] bool is_valid_port(int port) const noexcept {
        return port > 0 && port <= 65535;
    }

    // DO: Comparison operators
    bool operator==(const UserService& other) const noexcept = default;
};


// === What happens if noexcept function throws ===

void dangerous() noexcept {
    // If this throws, std::terminate() is called IMMEDIATELY
    // No stack unwinding, no cleanup, no catch handlers
    // throw std::runtime_error("oops");  // -> std::terminate!
}


// === Summary ===
// Mark noexcept:
//   - Move constructor/assignment (CRITICAL for performance)
//   - Swap functions
//   - Destructors (already implicit)
//   - Simple getters, size(), empty(), data()
//   - Comparison operators
//   - Hash functions
//
// Don't mark noexcept:
//   - Functions that allocate memory
//   - Functions that do I/O
//   - Functions that call potentially-throwing code
//   - Virtual functions (derived class might need to throw)
//   - Functions whose implementation might change

int main() {
    demo_vector_optimization();
    return 0;
}
```

instruction: Show how to implement custom error categories in C++ using std::error_code and std::error_category for library error handling.
output:
<think>
std::error_code and std::error_category are C++11's answer to the problem of portable, extensible error codes. The system:

1. error_code: A (int, error_category*) pair. The int is the error value, the category gives it meaning.
2. error_category: A singleton that names and describes errors for a domain.
3. error_condition: Portable error conditions (like POSIX errc) for cross-platform matching.

Why use this instead of exceptions or simple enums?
- Library boundaries: error_code is copyable, doesn't require exception support
- Composition: different libraries have different error spaces, categories keep them separate
- Standard integration: works with std::system_error, std::filesystem, networking TS
- Pattern matching: compare error_codes against error_conditions portably

To implement:
1. Define an enum for your errors
2. Create a singleton error_category subclass
3. Specialize std::is_error_code_enum to enable implicit conversion
4. Provide make_error_code overload
</think>

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <string>
#include <system_error>

// === Step 1: Define error enum ===

enum class DatabaseError {
    Ok = 0,  // 0 must mean success
    ConnectionFailed = 1,
    QueryFailed = 2,
    Timeout = 3,
    AuthFailed = 4,
    NotFound = 5,
    ConstraintViolation = 6,
};

// === Step 2: Implement error_category ===

class DatabaseErrorCategory : public std::error_category {
public:
    const char* name() const noexcept override {
        return "database";
    }

    std::string message(int ev) const override {
        switch (static_cast<DatabaseError>(ev)) {
            case DatabaseError::Ok: return "success";
            case DatabaseError::ConnectionFailed: return "connection failed";
            case DatabaseError::QueryFailed: return "query execution failed";
            case DatabaseError::Timeout: return "operation timed out";
            case DatabaseError::AuthFailed: return "authentication failed";
            case DatabaseError::NotFound: return "record not found";
            case DatabaseError::ConstraintViolation: return "constraint violation";
            default: return "unknown database error";
        }
    }

    // Map to standard error conditions for portable comparison
    std::error_condition default_error_condition(int ev) const noexcept override {
        switch (static_cast<DatabaseError>(ev)) {
            case DatabaseError::AuthFailed:
                return std::errc::permission_denied;
            case DatabaseError::Timeout:
                return std::errc::timed_out;
            case DatabaseError::NotFound:
                return std::errc::no_such_file_or_directory;
            default:
                return std::error_condition(ev, *this);
        }
    }
};

// Singleton accessor
inline const DatabaseErrorCategory& database_category() {
    static DatabaseErrorCategory instance;
    return instance;
}

// === Step 3: Enable implicit conversion ===

namespace std {
    template<>
    struct is_error_code_enum<DatabaseError> : true_type {};
}

// make_error_code overload (ADL-discoverable)
std::error_code make_error_code(DatabaseError e) {
    return {static_cast<int>(e), database_category()};
}


// === Step 4: Use in library API ===

struct QueryResult {
    std::string data;
};

// Return error_code for non-throwing API
std::error_code execute_query(
    const std::string& sql,
    QueryResult& result
) {
    if (sql.empty()) {
        return DatabaseError::QueryFailed;
    }
    if (sql.find("DROP") != std::string::npos) {
        return DatabaseError::AuthFailed;
    }
    result.data = "query result for: " + sql;
    return {};  // Default error_code = success
}

// Or use std::expected (C++23) for modern API
// std::expected<QueryResult, std::error_code> execute(const std::string& sql);


// === Step 5: Consumer code ===

void demo() {
    QueryResult result;
    std::error_code ec = execute_query("", result);

    if (ec) {  // Non-zero = error
        std::cout << "Error: " << ec.message() << "\n";
        std::cout << "Category: " << ec.category().name() << "\n";
        std::cout << "Code: " << ec.value() << "\n";

        // Portable comparison via error_condition
        if (ec == std::errc::permission_denied) {
            std::cout << "Access denied (portable check)\n";
        }

        // Specific check via enum
        if (ec == DatabaseError::QueryFailed) {
            std::cout << "Query failed (specific check)\n";
        }
    }

    // Can also throw as system_error
    ec = execute_query("DROP TABLE users", result);
    if (ec) {
        // throw std::system_error(ec, "while executing query");
        std::cout << "Would throw: " << ec.message() << "\n";
    }
}

// === Composing error categories ===
// Each library defines its own category. They never collide because
// error_code compares both value AND category pointer.

enum class CacheError { Ok = 0, Miss = 1, Full = 2, Expired = 3 };

class CacheErrorCategory : public std::error_category {
public:
    const char* name() const noexcept override { return "cache"; }
    std::string message(int ev) const override {
        switch (static_cast<CacheError>(ev)) {
            case CacheError::Ok: return "success";
            case CacheError::Miss: return "cache miss";
            case CacheError::Full: return "cache full";
            case CacheError::Expired: return "entry expired";
            default: return "unknown cache error";
        }
    }
};

// DatabaseError::NotFound (5) != CacheError::Expired (3)
// even if values accidentally matched — different categories

int main() {
    demo();
    return 0;
}
```

instruction: Show the std::expected pattern in C++23 for functional error handling with monadic operations like and_then, transform, and or_else.
output:
<think>
std::expected (C++23) brings Rust-like Result semantics to C++. It holds either a value T or an error E. The key monadic operations:

1. transform (map): Apply function to value, pass error through
2. and_then (flatMap): Chain operations that might fail
3. or_else: Handle/recover from errors
4. transform_error: Transform the error type

These enable functional-style error handling pipelines without exceptions. The value_or method provides defaults.

std::expected is different from std::optional because:
- optional: value or nothing (no error info)
- expected: value or typed error (preserves error details)

I'll show a practical pipeline: parse config file, validate, use — all with expected chaining.
</think>

```cpp
#include <expected>
#include <string>
#include <charconv>
#include <fstream>
#include <iostream>
#include <sstream>
#include <unordered_map>
#include <functional>

// Error type
struct AppError {
    enum Kind { FileNotFound, ParseError, ValidationError, NetworkError };
    Kind kind;
    std::string message;

    static AppError file_error(const std::string& path) {
        return {FileNotFound, "Cannot open: " + path};
    }
    static AppError parse_error(const std::string& detail) {
        return {ParseError, detail};
    }
    static AppError validation_error(const std::string& detail) {
        return {ValidationError, detail};
    }
};

struct Config {
    std::string host;
    int port;
    int max_connections;
};

using ConfigMap = std::unordered_map<std::string, std::string>;

// === Building blocks: each returns expected ===

std::expected<std::string, AppError>
read_file(const std::string& path) {
    std::ifstream file(path);
    if (!file.is_open()) {
        return std::unexpected(AppError::file_error(path));
    }
    std::ostringstream ss;
    ss << file.rdbuf();
    return ss.str();
}

std::expected<ConfigMap, AppError>
parse_ini(const std::string& content) {
    ConfigMap map;
    std::istringstream stream(content);
    std::string line;
    int line_num = 0;

    while (std::getline(stream, line)) {
        ++line_num;
        if (line.empty() || line[0] == '#') continue;
        auto eq = line.find('=');
        if (eq == std::string::npos) {
            return std::unexpected(AppError::parse_error(
                "Line " + std::to_string(line_num) + ": missing '='"
            ));
        }
        map[line.substr(0, eq)] = line.substr(eq + 1);
    }
    return map;
}

std::expected<int, AppError>
parse_int(const std::string& s, const std::string& field) {
    int value;
    auto [ptr, ec] = std::from_chars(s.data(), s.data() + s.size(), value);
    if (ec != std::errc{}) {
        return std::unexpected(AppError::parse_error(
            field + ": '" + s + "' is not a valid integer"
        ));
    }
    return value;
}

std::expected<Config, AppError>
build_config(const ConfigMap& map) {
    auto host_it = map.find("host");
    if (host_it == map.end()) {
        return std::unexpected(AppError::validation_error("missing 'host'"));
    }

    auto port_it = map.find("port");
    if (port_it == map.end()) {
        return std::unexpected(AppError::validation_error("missing 'port'"));
    }

    auto port = parse_int(port_it->second, "port");
    if (!port) return std::unexpected(port.error());

    auto max_conn_it = map.find("max_connections");
    int max_conn = 100;  // default
    if (max_conn_it != map.end()) {
        auto parsed = parse_int(max_conn_it->second, "max_connections");
        if (!parsed) return std::unexpected(parsed.error());
        max_conn = *parsed;
    }

    return Config{host_it->second, *port, max_conn};
}

std::expected<Config, AppError>
validate_config(Config cfg) {
    if (cfg.port < 1 || cfg.port > 65535) {
        return std::unexpected(AppError::validation_error(
            "port " + std::to_string(cfg.port) + " out of range"
        ));
    }
    if (cfg.max_connections < 1) {
        return std::unexpected(AppError::validation_error(
            "max_connections must be positive"
        ));
    }
    return cfg;
}

// === Monadic chaining pipeline ===

std::expected<Config, AppError>
load_config(const std::string& path) {
    return read_file(path)
        .and_then(parse_ini)       // string -> expected<ConfigMap>
        .and_then(build_config)    // ConfigMap -> expected<Config>
        .and_then(validate_config);// Config -> expected<Config>
}

// === Using transform (map over success value) ===

std::expected<int, AppError>
load_port(const std::string& path) {
    return load_config(path)
        .transform([](const Config& c) { return c.port; });
}

// === Using or_else (error recovery) ===

std::expected<Config, AppError>
load_config_with_fallback(const std::string& path) {
    return load_config(path)
        .or_else([](const AppError& err) -> std::expected<Config, AppError> {
            if (err.kind == AppError::FileNotFound) {
                // Fallback to defaults
                return Config{"localhost", 8080, 100};
            }
            return std::unexpected(err);  // Re-propagate other errors
        });
}

// === Using transform_error (convert error types) ===

std::expected<Config, std::string>
load_config_string_error(const std::string& path) {
    return load_config(path)
        .transform_error([](const AppError& e) { return e.message; });
}

int main() {
    auto config = load_config_with_fallback("app.conf");
    if (config) {
        std::cout << "Host: " << config->host
                  << " Port: " << config->port
                  << " MaxConn: " << config->max_connections << "\n";
    } else {
        std::cerr << "Error: " << config.error().message << "\n";
    }

    // value_or for quick defaults
    int port = load_port("app.conf").value_or(8080);
    std::cout << "Port: " << port << "\n";

    return 0;
}
```
